text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
In the previous study, we saw that the Kohathites were given no wagons and no oxen. While the other Levite clans received carts to carry the outer coverings and structural frames of the tabernacle, the Kohathites were given something different, holy responsibility and human shoulders. They were chosen to carry the most sacred objects of God’s dwelling personally, on their own bodies, step by step through the wilderness. Today we look more closely at what exactly they carried, and why the number of those items carries its own spiritual message for us.

According to Numbers 4:4-15, the Kohathites carried six main sacred objects, along with the holy utensils and instruments connected to each one.
The six items were: the Ark of the Covenant, the Table of Showbread,
the Lampstand, the Golden Altar of Incense, the Bronze Altar of Burnt Offering, and the general utensils of the sanctuary ministry. Each of these came with its own set of vessels. For the Table of Showbread, they carried the dishes, pans, bowls, and pitchers. For the Lampstand, they carried the lamps, wick trimmers, trays, and oil jars. For the Bronze Altar, they carried the firepans, fleshhooks, shovels, basins and then adds that all the other instruments of sanctuary ministry were also included. Every holy tool had a carrier. Every sacred vessel had a shoulder assigned to it.

One thing is worth noticing here. The Bronze Laver, which stood between the Bronze Altar and the entrance of the tabernacle, is not mentioned anywhere in Numbers 4. The chapter clearly names every other item, but the laver is noticeably absent. Scripture gives no specific instruction about who carried it or how. This silence is not a mistake. God is precise in what He records and in what He withholds. Why the Bible is silent about the laver is a question we will bring to study in the coming days. For now, we set our attention on the six items that are clearly named, and what they are teaching us.

In the Bible, the number six is consistently connected with man, and especially with man in his earthly and unfinished condition. God created man on the sixth day, as we read in Genesis 1:26-31. Because of this, six also means imperfect humans; man as creature, man in weakness, man who has been made by God but has not yet arrived at the full image and likeness, rest and completeness that God has designed for him. Six is always one step short of seven, and seven in Scripture is the number of God’s completion, His rest, and His perfection. Man at six is genuine, real, and created with dignity, but he is not yet finished. He is on the way.

When we come to the New Testament, we find this same language speaking again. Our Lord Jesus Christ, who came to redeem fallen mankind, hung upon the cross for six hours; from the third hour to the ninth hour, as recorded in Mark 15:25 and 33. Six hours for a sixth-day man. The measure of His suffering matched the measure of our fall. God did not send a quick rescue. His Son bore the full weight of man’s condition hour by hour, until the work was finished and He could cry “It is finished” from the cross. The six hours and the six holy items the Kohathites carried on their shoulders are speaking the same language. Holy things were borne on human shoulders for the sake of fallen man.
The cross and the wilderness journey both carry this same truth.

The Kohathites were not angels. They were not glorified saints standing in heavenly perfection. They were dusty, finite, wilderness-walking men.
They had limitations, weaknesses, and the same human frailty that every one of us carries. And yet God entrusted to their shoulders the most holy objects in the universe; the Ark that housed His presence, the Lampstand that held His light, the Altar where atonement was made. All of it was placed on imperfect human shoulders. This is one of the most astonishing truths in all of Scripture. God deliberately chooses the unfinished to carry the finished. He places perfect treasure in imperfect vessels, not because He has no other choice, but because this is His sovereign design. When the glory arrives through a weak vessel,
no one can credit the vessel. The glory belongs entirely to God.

This is exactly what Paul understood when he wrote in 2 Corinthians 4:7,
“We have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellence of the power may be of God and not of us.” The vessel is weak, but the treasure is holy. The man is limited, but the burden is divine. The carrier is human, but what he carries belongs to God. Paul was not embarrassed by his weakness. He understood it as part of God’s deliberate design. And we also read in 1 Corinthians 1:27 that God chose what is weak in the world to carry out what is great in His purpose. The Kohathites are a living picture of this truth. Six holy burdens placed on shoulders that were still being formed.

This does not mean that God approves of carelessness or takes human imperfection lightly. The Kohathites were not allowed to carry carelessly.
The order was strict. The covering had to be done first. The priests had to prepare everything before the Kohathites could even come near. If they touched the holy things without the proper covering, they would die. So, the truth is not that God is relaxed about holiness; it is that God, in His mercy, takes man as he is and gives him holy responsibility, while at the same time calling him upward. He entrusts us with what is holy in order to train us through the carrying.
God did not wait until the Kohathites were perfected before He gave them the holy things. He gave them the holy things as part of the very process of making them holy.

Man was created on the sixth day, but he was created for God. The six items being carried on Kohathite shoulders preach a quiet but powerful message: human life finds its true purpose only when it bears what belongs to God.
Man is not fulfilled by carrying his own plans, his own ambitions, or his own agenda through the wilderness of this world. He becomes truly meaningful when he carries divine responsibility. The six holy burdens remind us that fallen man is not abandoned by God. He is redeemed, entrusted, and trained through the burden itself. The weight of what we carry for God is not meant to crush us. It is meant to shape us.

For us as new creatures today, we are the Kohathites of the new covenant.
We are men and women of the sixth day; still in the wilderness, still being formed, not yet glorified, not yet arrived at the final rest. And yet God has placed holy things on our shoulders. He has entrusted us with His truth,
His testimony, His gospel, His presence in prayer and worship and witness.
We carry these not because we are already complete, but because God is working through us and shaping us as we carry. The burden is perfect, but the carrier is still being formed. And that is the wonder of grace. God does not wait for perfect men. He works through willing ones.

In the studies ahead, we will look at each of these six items one by one; what each one represents, what it points to in Christ, and what it means for us to carry it today on our shoulders. Each item has a spiritual depth that will take time and care to open up. What we have seen today is simply this: six holy burdens were placed on human shoulders, because God has always worked this way. The carrier is weak, but the calling is holy. The vessel is earthly,
but the burden is heavenly. Prepare your heart for a long and rich journey through the Kohathites.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, you are the God who places holy things on human shoulders. You do not wait for perfect men before You give holy responsibility. You take what is weak and You work through it for Your glory. We thank You that You have not abandoned fallen man but have redeemed him, called him, and entrusted him with what is divine. Forgive us for the times we have carried Your things carelessly or set them down because the weight felt too much. Teach us to carry with reverence, with order, and with obedience, as the Kohathites were called to do. Let us not handle Your truth, Your presence, and Your gospel as common things. Help us to remember that what we carry belongs to You, and that the glory must always return to You alone. Form us through the carrying, and keep us faithful through the wilderness, until we arrive at the rest You have prepared for us. In Christ’s name, amen.
